title: 1.1: Explains why the timing measurements show linear or non linear scaling behavior (e.g., processing time scales proportionally with input size, no exponential blowup observed, time remains in milliseconds for large inputs)
1.2: Reports throughput measurements comparing normal vs adversarial content (e.g., MB/sec processing rates)
1.3: Shows CPU profiling with pprof command execution and function-level breakdown output (e.g., go tool pprof -top showing flat%, cum%, function names)
1.4: Reports conclusion on whether computational complexity attacks are feasible (e.g., not vulnerable, attacks ineffective, resource exhaustion not possible)
1.5: Shows testing of adversarial input patterns designed to trigger backtracking (e.g., nested patterns, near-matches, malicious key formats)
1.6: Reports timing measurements for TruffleHog's actual named detector patterns (e.g., privatekey, aws_key, twilio patterns with individual timing data per pattern)
1.7: Reports timing comparison between normal and adversarial inputs of the same byte size (e.g., 1024 KB normal vs 1024 KB adversarial content)
